parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 cumulative diff introduces critical infrastructure (retry, error dispatch, job tracking) but carries three material risks that should block shipment: (1) `scripts/lib/process.mjs` was deleted entirely (67 lines removed) — if any surviving import references it, the entire companion crashes at module-load time with no recovery; (2) `scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs` absorbed 155 lines of retry/backoff integration yet has zero unit-test coverage, meaning the boundary most exposed to real failures (external API) is validated only on the happy path; (3) `scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs` was rewritten (78 lines) with no corresponding test, risking duplicate or lost job submissions under concurrent or retry scenarios.

findings.title: Deleted process.mjs may leave dangling imports causing unrecoverable runtime crash
findings.body: The diff removes `scripts/lib/process.mjs` entirely (67 lines, no additions). The companion codebase has multiple modules that could still `import` from this path — notably glm-client.mjs, glm-companion.mjs, and session-lifecycle-hook.mjs, all of which were modified in this same diff. If any surviving `import ... from './process.mjs'` or `from './lib/process.mjs'` exists, Node.js will throw `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` at startup for every invocation. This class of failure is invisible to unit tests if the test runner mocks or stubs the import chain, and it produces zero diagnostic output beyond a stack trace. The `scripts/check-imports.mjs` file was altered by only 5 lines — insufficient confidence that it validates this deletion.
findings.recommendation: Grep the entire codebase for any remaining reference to 'process.mjs' or 'lib/process'. Add an explicit CI guard in check-imports.mjs that fails if any source file imports from a deleted module. Consider a smoke test that actually requires glm-companion.mjs and verifies it loads without error.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs has 155 lines of retry/backoff integration with no unit test
findings.body: glm-client.mjs is the sole boundary to the external BigModel API. In this diff it absorbs the new retry.mjs and bigmodel-errors.mjs modules — 155 lines changed. The test directory contains retry.test.mjs and bigmodel-errors.test.mjs, but no `glm-client.test.mjs`. This means the integration point where retry classification meets actual HTTP requests is completely untested. Failure modes that unit tests on retry.mjs alone cannot catch: wrong error shape passed to classify(), retry headers not forwarded, response body consumed before retry can re-read it, or backoff timers stacking on top of the client's own timeout. Any of these would manifest as silent no-ops or hung requests in production.
findings.recommendation: Add a `tests/glm-client.test.mjs` that exercises at minimum: (a) a request that triggers a retriable BigModel error and verifies the retry path fires; (b) a request that exhausts retries and verifies the final error propagates to the caller; (c) a concurrent-submission scenario that validates retry state does not leak across in-flight requests.

findings.title: tracked-jobs.mjs rewritten (78 lines) with no test — job duplication or loss risk
findings.body: tracked-jobs.mjs manages persistent job state for background and wait-mode execution. It was substantially modified (78 lines in the diff) but has no dedicated test file. Under the --background/--wait execution modes introduced in v0.4.5, a race between job registration, status polling, and retry-driven re-submission could cause either duplicate submissions to the API (wasting tokens, producing duplicate outputs) or a job being marked complete before its result is recorded (silent data loss). The job-render.test.mjs file exists but appears to test rendering, not job lifecycle state transitions.
findings.recommendation: Add a `tests/tracked-jobs.test.mjs` covering: (a) idempotent registration — calling register twice with the same job ID does not create duplicates; (b) concurrent status transitions — a job marked complete while a retry is in-flight does not corrupt state; (c) crash-recovery — a job in 'running' state at process exit is correctly handled on next invocation.

next_steps: Run `grep -r 'process\.mjs' scripts/` from repo root and confirm zero hits before merge.
Create `tests/glm-client.test.mjs` with minimum viable coverage for retry integration (retriable error, exhausted retries, concurrent requests).
Create `tests/tracked-jobs.test.mjs` with idempotency and crash-recovery scenarios.
Add a CI-level smoke test that `node -e 'import("./scripts/glm-companion.mjs")'` succeeds without throwing, catching module-load failures early.
